svg.text: BFS vs. DFS — When Each Wins
BFS — Queue, level-by-level
• Shortest path in unweighted graphs
• Layered traversal — "k steps from source"
• Multi-source BFS — push all sources, expand
• Bidirectional BFS — meet-in-the-middle
Mark visited at push time, not pop time.
Otherwise nodes get re-pushed and complexity
degenerates to exponential.
Time: O(V + E) Space: O(V) for the queue
Levels measured by tracking depth on each node
or processing the queue size at each round.
Pitfall: weighted graph
⇒ BFS gives wrong answer; use Dijkstra.
0-1 BFS uses a deque to handle 0/1 weights.
DFS — Recursion or stack
• Connected components, cycle detection
• Topological sort via post-order
• Strongly connected components (Tarjan)
• Tree problems with parent state
• Backtracking on the graph (count paths)
Three colours for cycle detection: white
(unseen), grey (in stack), black (done).
Time: O(V + E) Space: O(V) recursion stack
Iterative DFS replaces recursion with explicit
stack — required for very deep graphs.
Pitfall: shortest path on weighted graph
DFS finds a path, not the shortest path.
Use Dijkstra or BFS instead.
